h1Title: Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is a compressive neuropathy of the posterior tibial nerve as it passes through the tarsal tunnel — a fibro-osseous canal beneath the flexor retinaculum on the medial aspect of the ankle. It is the foot's anatomical equivalent of carpal tunnel syndrome of the wrist. The posterior tibial nerve supplies sensation to the plantar foot and intrinsic foot muscles; compression produces burning, tingling, and numbness along the plantar surface, heel, and toes. True idiopathic tarsal tunnel syndrome is uncommon; most cases have an identifiable cause such as space-occupying lesion, flat-foot deformity, post-traumatic fibrosis, or systemic neuropathy. Diagnosis requires nerve conduction studies for confirmation; treatment ranges from orthotics and steroid injection for mild cases to surgical tibial nerve decompression for refractory compression.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact population prevalence is unknown; estimated 0.5-1% of chronic foot and ankle pain referrals; accounts for approximately 1% of lower-extremity nerve entrapments in specialty clinics
keyStats.demographics: Affects adults at any age; women slightly more often than men in most surgical series; associated with flat foot, which has female predominance over 40
keyStats.avgAge: Peak presentation in adults aged 30-60; juvenile tarsal tunnel occurs in children with tarsal coalition or flat feet
keyStats.globalCases: Prevalence data limited; tarsal tunnel is under-recognized and frequently misdiagnosed as plantar fasciitis in primary care

definition: Tarsal tunnel syndrome (ICD-10: G57.5) is entrapment or compression of the posterior tibial nerve within the tarsal tunnel, a fibro-osseous space bounded medially by the flexor retinaculum (laciniate ligament), posteriorly and inferiorly by the medial surfaces of the calcaneus, talus, and medial malleolus. Within the tunnel, the nerve divides into three terminal branches: the medial plantar nerve (sensation to the medial three-and-a-half toes and plantar forefoot), the lateral plantar nerve (sensation to the lateral toes and plantar heel), and the medial calcaneal branch (sensation to the medial and plantar heel). Any mass lesion, swelling, or structural change that reduces tunnel volume can compress these branches, producing sensory symptoms in their specific distributions. The condition is heterogeneous: proximal tarsal tunnel syndrome involves the main tibial nerve trunk; distal (or 'jogger's foot') involves only the medial plantar branch. Space-occupying lesions (ganglia, lipomas, varicose veins, accessory muscles, tenosynovitis of the FHL or FDL tendons) account for approximately 60% of surgical series cases. Structural deformity — particularly hindfoot valgus in flat feet — stretches or impinges the nerve through altered tunnel geometry. Post-traumatic fibrosis after ankle sprain or fracture is a common cause. Podiatrists, neurologists, and foot and ankle surgeons collaborate in management, with podiatrists typically providing the first evaluation and conservative management.

typesClassification.description: No identifiable compressive lesion or structural deformity. Fibrosis of the flexor retinaculum may be the mechanism. Responds to conservative treatment in 50-60% of cases; outcomes of surgical decompression are less predictable than in lesion-driven cases.

A ganglion cyst, lipoma, varicose vein, accessory muscle (flexor digitorum accessorius longus), FHL tenosynovitis, or bone spur within the tunnel compresses the nerve. Surgical excision of the lesion plus retinacular release gives the best outcomes among all subtypes.
Fibrosis, adhesions, or bony deformity following calcaneal fracture, ankle fracture, or severe ankle sprain impinges the nerve. Outcome of surgery depends on extent of post-traumatic changes.

Hindfoot valgus and medial arch collapse stretch and angulate the posterior tibial nerve within the tunnel. Commonly bilateral. Correction of the flat-foot deformity (orthotics or reconstructive surgery) is a key component of management.

typesClassification.type: Distal tarsal tunnel syndrome (medial plantar nerve entrapment)
typesClassification.description: Selective compression of the medial plantar branch — also called 'jogger's foot' — causes numbness and burning in the medial forefoot and big toe without involvement of the heel or lateral plantar distribution. Associated with hyperpronation in runners.

primarySymptoms: Burning, tingling, or electric-shock sensation along the plantar surface of the foot, extending from the medial heel across the plantar arch to the toes — distributed in the territory of the medial and lateral plantar nerves.
Numbness or reduced sensation affecting the plantar foot and toes, often described as 'walking on a pebble' or 'feeling the floor through a thick sock'.
Medial ankle and heel pain, located posterior and inferior to the medial malleolus, aggravated by prolonged standing, walking, or the end of a work day.
Positive Tinel sign at the tarsal tunnel — a reproduction of burning or tingling along the plantar foot when the posterior tibial nerve is tapped directly behind and below the medial malleolus.
Pain and neurological symptoms that worsen with activity and improve with rest and elevation of the foot, a pattern consistent with increased tunnel pressure during weight-bearing.

Night pain and nocturnal tingling, particularly after a day of prolonged activity — distinguished from plantar fasciitis by bilateral or plantar-distributed symptoms rather than isolated heel-strike pain.

Weakness and cramping of the intrinsic foot muscles in advanced compression — the flexor hallucis brevis and abductor hallucis are supplied by the medial plantar nerve; wasting may be visible in severe chronic cases.
Radiation of pain proximally up the medial calf ('double crush' phenomenon or retrograde pain from an entrapped nerve, particularly in cases with coexisting lumbar radiculopathy).
earlyWarningSigns: Occasional burning in the sole of the foot after prolonged walking that resolves with rest — intermittent nerve ischemia in mild compression before permanent axonal changes develop.
Mild tingling in the plantar forefoot after running or long walks that disappears within 30 minutes of ceasing activity.
Vague medial ankle discomfort that the patient attributes to ankle 'weakness' rather than a specific neurological sensation.
New onset of nocturnal foot tingling in a patient with known flat feet or recent ankle sprain — a combination raising suspicion for structural tarsal tunnel compression.
emergencySigns: Acute foot drop with loss of intrinsic foot function or complete plantar foot anaesthesia — suggests severe compression, nerve transaction, or an expanding space-occupying lesion requiring emergency imaging and surgical consultation.

Progressive weakness of great-toe flexion combined with worsening numbness — indicates significant axonal loss requiring urgent nerve conduction studies and expedited surgical decompression before irreversible motor damage.

Acute tarsal tunnel symptoms with swelling, bruising, and medial ankle tenderness after trauma — may indicate posterior tibial nerve injury from calcaneal or ankle fracture requiring urgent orthopedic or podiatric assessment.

causes.cause: Space-occupying lesion within the tarsal tunnel
causes.description: Ganglia are the most common single cause, found in 25-40% of surgical series. Varicose veins, lipomas, anomalous FHL tenosynovitis, accessory muscles, and posterior tibial bone spurs each account for smaller proportions. Any mass within the fixed tunnel volume compresses the posterior tibial nerve and its branches.

causes.cause: Hindfoot valgus and flat-foot deformity (pes planus)
causes.description: Excessive pronation elongates the tarsal tunnel's posterior wall, stretching the tibial nerve as it curves around the medial malleolus. The nerve experiences both tensile and compressive forces during the hyperpronated gait cycle. Correction of hindfoot valgus with orthotics or surgery is a key treatment component.

Ankle sprains, calcaneal fractures, and tibial or talar fractures produce peritendinous and perineural fibrosis within the tarsal tunnel. Adhesions tether the nerve, limiting its normal excursion during foot movement and causing dynamic compression with every step.

Rheumatoid arthritis and other inflammatory arthritides produce tenosynovitis of the FHL and FDL tendons within the tarsal tunnel, generating a synovitis-driven mass effect on the tibial nerve. Tarsal tunnel syndrome in RA is often bilateral and correlates with disease activity.

causes.cause: Varicosities and vascular anomalies of the tarsal tunnel
causes.description: Dilated posterior tibial veins within the tunnel may enlarge with prolonged dependency and standing, compressing the nerve dynamically. This explains the classic symptom pattern of worsening symptoms toward the end of a working day and relief with elevation.

Approximately 20-25% of surgical series find no identifiable causative lesion. Histopathology shows thickening and fibrosis of the flexor retinaculum producing intrinsic tunnel stenosis, analogous to carpal tunnel idiopathic fibrosis.

riskFactors.factor: Flat feet (pes planus) and hindfoot valgus
riskFactors.description: The most common structural risk factor, present in 40-50% of tarsal tunnel syndrome patients presenting for surgery. Excessive pronation changes tunnel geometry and nerve tension with every step.

Post-traumatic fibrosis within the tarsal tunnel following ankle ligament injury or calcaneal fracture is identified in approximately 15-20% of surgical cases. The interval from injury to symptom onset may be months to years.

riskFactors.factor: Occupations requiring prolonged standing or walking
riskFactors.description: Sustained weight-bearing increases tarsal tunnel pressure and venous congestion within the tunnel; symptom onset or worsening in jobs requiring more than 6 hours per day on feet is characteristic.
Increased axial loading amplifies dynamic tunnel pressure during weight-bearing; adipose proliferation in perineural tissue may also directly compress the nerve.
Tarsal tunnel involvement from tenosynovitis occurs in 10-15% of RA patients and is correlated with active disease and hindfoot deformity.

Diabetic peripheral neuropathy and tarsal tunnel syndrome coexist in a 'double crush' phenomenon — pre-existing neuropathic nerve is more vulnerable to focal compression. Outcomes of surgical decompression are better when the nerve retains residual function.

riskFactors.factor: Varicose veins of the lower extremity
riskFactors.description: Lower-extremity venous insufficiency may cause tarsal tunnel varicosities that expand with prolonged dependency; addressing venous hypertension reduces dynamic nerve compression in this subgroup.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 30-60 are the largest presenting group in specialist clinic series; tarsal tunnel syndrome is uncommon in children except those with tarsal coalition or severe flat feet.
Women are moderately over-represented in conservative and surgical treatment series, partly reflecting the female predominance of adult flat-foot deformity (PTTD) as an underlying cause.
Athletes — particularly distance runners and ballet dancers — develop the distal medial plantar nerve subtype from repetitive hyperpronation.
Patients with rheumatoid arthritis have approximately 10-15% prevalence of tarsal tunnel involvement as a manifestation of systemic disease.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of tarsal tunnel syndrome combines clinical evaluation with electrodiagnostic testing. The clinical history establishes the character, distribution, and aggravating factors of symptoms — burning, tingling, or numbness in the plantar foot with a weight-bearing-related pattern. Physical examination includes percussion of the posterior tibial nerve below the medial malleolus (Tinel's test) and sustained pressure over the tarsal tunnel for 30-60 seconds (compression test) — both should reproduce the patient's paresthetic symptoms to be considered positive. Sensory testing with Semmes-Weinstein monofilaments maps plantar deficit. Subtalar joint range of motion and flatfoot assessment identify structural compression. Nerve conduction studies (NCS) and electromyography (EMG) are the investigation standard: distal motor latency of the medial or lateral plantar nerve above 6.2 ms or absent sensory potentials across the tarsal tunnel confirm compressive neuropathy. Sensitivity of NCS is 60-90% in confirmed surgical cases. MRI of the ankle and tarsal tunnel is indicated when a space-occupying lesion is suspected and guides surgical planning — MRI identifies ganglia, varicosities, tenosynovitis, and accessory muscles with high sensitivity. Ultrasound is a useful adjunct for dynamic assessment of nerve caliber and for identifying vascular anomalies. Diagnostic nerve block with local anesthetic confirms the diagnosis when NCS is equivocal and provides short-term therapeutic benefit.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reproduces burning or tingling in the plantar foot distribution by tapping the tibial nerve at the tarsal tunnel; supports clinical diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician taps the skin behind the medial malleolus with a reflex hammer or finger. A positive test produces a radiating tingling sensation in the plantar foot. Takes 1-2 minutes; causes momentary discomfort in positive cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms compressive neuropathy of the medial or lateral plantar nerve; quantifies severity and distinguishes tarsal tunnel from peripheral neuropathy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrode pads placed on the plantar foot and ankle; brief electrical stimuli applied. EMG involves small needle electrodes in foot muscles. Mild discomfort during testing; session takes 30-60 minutes. Results reviewed by a neurologist or physiatrist.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of ankle and tarsal tunnel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies space-occupying lesions (ganglia, varicosities, lipomas, tenosynovitis) and assesses nerve signal changes; guides surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scan lying still; gadolinium contrast often used for soft-tissue lesion characterization. No radiation. Results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the nerve as the pain source and provides temporary therapeutic relief; helps predict surgical response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthetic injected near the posterior tibial nerve under the medial malleolus under sterile technique. Burning during injection; numbness in the plantar foot within 10-15 minutes for 2-4 hours. Discomfort minimal after local anesthesia takes effect.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps plantar sensory deficit, distinguishing medial plantar from lateral plantar branch involvement and tracking treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Nylon monofilaments of increasing diameter are touched to the plantar foot while the patient's eyes are closed; they report what they feel. Painless; takes 5-10 minutes per foot.

diagnosticTests.test: Ultrasound of tarsal tunnel and posterior tibial nerve
diagnosticTests.purpose: Real-time dynamic assessment of nerve caliber, vascularity, and adjacent masses; identifies varicosities that may not be apparent on MRI
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel applied to the medial ankle; probe moved along the nerve pathway. Painless; takes 15-20 minutes. Can be performed with weight-bearing or dynamic dorsiflexion to assess positional compression.

treatmentOverview: Treatment strategy is guided by the underlying cause, symptom severity, and electrodiagnostic findings. Conservative treatment is first-line for mild-to-moderate symptoms and should be maintained for at least 3-6 months before surgical consideration. The cornerstone of non-surgical management is addressing the biomechanical causes: custom foot orthotics with medial hindfoot posting correct flat-foot-driven nerve tension and relieve approximately 40% of tarsal tunnel pressure in valgus-driven cases. Activity modification reduces prolonged weight-bearing that elevates tunnel pressure. NSAIDs address inflammatory tenosynovitis within the tunnel. Corticosteroid injection (40 mg methylprednisolone with local anesthetic) into the tarsal tunnel — not directly into the nerve — reduces perineural inflammation and edema; approximately 50-60% of patients experience meaningful relief lasting 2-4 months, and the response to injection is a useful predictor of surgical success. A short-course below-knee walking boot for 4-6 weeks rests the tendon structures within the tunnel in inflammatory cases. Physiotherapy targeting intrinsic foot and calf strengthening supports biomechanical correction. Neuropathic analgesics (gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, amitriptyline) manage the neurogenic pain component during conservative treatment. When conservative management fails after 3-6 months of optimal care, or when a specific compressive lesion is identified on MRI, surgical decompression is indicated. Tibial nerve decompression involves division of the flexor retinaculum and, where a lesion is present, its excision. Outcomes are best when a specific causative lesion can be removed — good-to-excellent results in 80-90% of lesion-positive cases versus 50-70% in idiopathic cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Custom foot orthotics with medial heel post
medicalTreatments.description: Controls hindfoot valgus and reduces the elongating tensile force on the tibial nerve during the pronation phase of gait. UCBL or Arizona brace provides more aggressive valgus control when significant flat-foot deformity drives compression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces symptoms in 40-60% of structural (flat-foot-related) tarsal tunnel syndrome cases; objective reduction in tarsal tunnel pressure has been measured in cadaveric studies with valgus correction

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone 40 mg with 1% lidocaine) into the tarsal tunnel
medicalTreatments.description: Injected into the tarsal tunnel canal adjacent to but not into the posterior tibial nerve to reduce perineural inflammation. Ultrasound guidance improves accuracy and safety.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meaningful pain relief (≥50% reduction) in approximately 50-60% of patients; duration 6-12 weeks; positive response predicts favorable surgical outcome

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg twice daily; ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce tenosynovitis of adjacent FHL and FDL tendons that contributes to tunnel volume reduction. Short courses (7-14 days) appropriate during inflammatory flares.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides symptomatic relief during acute inflammatory phases; no evidence for long-term structural improvement

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin (300-900 mg three times daily) or pregabalin (75-150 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Neuropathic analgesics targeting the central sensitization and neurogenic pain components of tarsal tunnel syndrome. Most effective when burning, allodynia, or night pain are the dominant symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces neuropathic pain scores by 30-50% in compressive neuropathy; titrated slowly to minimize sedation and dizziness side-effects

medicalTreatments.name: Below-knee walking boot immobilization (4-6 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces dynamic tension on the tibial nerve and rests inflammatory tenosynovitis of adjacent tendon sheaths. Used during acute symptom exacerbations or as a bridge to surgical decision-making.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom reduction in 40-60% of acute inflammatory cases; used as adjunct, not definitive treatment

surgicalOptions.name: Tibial nerve decompression (tarsal tunnel release)
surgicalOptions.description: Division of the flexor retinaculum from the medial malleolus to the abductor hallucis fascia under general or regional anesthesia. Space-occupying lesions are excised simultaneously. The medial and lateral plantar nerve branches and calcaneal branch are individually explored and any adhesions released.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good-to-excellent outcomes in 80-90% of lesion-driven cases; 50-70% in idiopathic cases; best predictor of success is relief from preoperative diagnostic nerve block

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic tarsal tunnel release
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive technique using a 2-portal endoscopic approach to divide the retinaculum. Smaller incisions, faster return to activity, lower wound complication rate compared with open release. Limited to cases without complex space-occupying lesions requiring open exploration.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable neurological outcomes to open release in selected cases; return to full activity at 6-8 weeks versus 10-12 weeks for open release in published series

surgicalOptions.name: Excision of space-occupying lesion (ganglion, varicosity, accessory muscle)
surgicalOptions.description: Targeted removal of the compressive lesion combined with retinacular release. Varicose vein clusters within the tunnel are ligated and excised; ganglia are traced to their origin and excised at base; accessory muscles are resected.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Lesion-targeted surgery gives the best results: over 85% good-to-excellent outcomes at 2 years in most surgical series

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) perineural injection
alternativeTreatments.description: PRP injected adjacent to the posterior tibial nerve provides growth factors that may reduce perineural fibrosis and nerve inflammation. Case series show symptom improvement at 3 months comparable to corticosteroid injection; evidence insufficient for routine recommendation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-level laser therapy (LLLT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Class III/IV laser applied over the tarsal tunnel region may reduce perineural inflammation and nociceptor sensitization. Small randomized studies show modest VAS score improvement over 4-6 weeks. Used as adjunct to conservative care in pain-management-focused protocols.

whySeeSpecialist: A podiatrist or foot and ankle surgeon should evaluate medial ankle or plantar foot burning and tingling that has persisted for more than 4-6 weeks, particularly when symptoms worsen with prolonged standing and improve with rest. Electrodiagnostic testing is required to confirm nerve entrapment and should be arranged by a neurologist or physiatrist before surgical decisions are made.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a podiatric surgeon or orthopedic foot and ankle surgeon who regularly performs tarsal tunnel decompression and has access to MRI for lesion identification. Confirm the practice has nerve conduction studies available (in-house or by referral) for preoperative diagnosis confirmation. Surgeons performing MRI-guided ultrasound injection for diagnostic nerve blocks provide an additional predictive tool before committing to surgery.
hospitalCriteria: Access to nerve conduction studies and EMG (neurologist or physiatrist)
MRI of the ankle for space-occupying lesion identification
Foot and ankle surgical unit with peripheral nerve decompression experience
Ultrasound-guided injection capability for diagnostic and therapeutic nerve blocks
keyFacilities: Podiatric and orthopedic foot and ankle surgical departments
preventionStrategies: Treat flat feet with custom orthotics to control hindfoot valgus and reduce dynamic tibial nerve tension within the tarsal tunnel.
Wear well-fitted, supportive footwear that prevents excessive pronation during occupations involving prolonged standing.
Manage ankle sprains and fractures with appropriate rehabilitation to minimize post-traumatic fibrosis in the tarsal tunnel region.
Control body weight to reduce axial loading and perineural adipose pressure within the tarsal tunnel.
Treat varicose veins and venous insufficiency of the lower extremity to reduce dynamic tunnel congestion from enlarged perineural veins.
lifestyleModifications: Wear prescribed custom orthotics with medial heel posting in all footwear throughout the day to correct the biomechanical driver of nerve tension.
Reduce prolonged standing; take regular breaks with foot elevation above heart level to reduce venous congestion in the tarsal tunnel.
Apply ice to the medial ankle for 15-20 minutes after activities that exacerbate symptoms.
Perform calf-muscle stretching (30 seconds, 3 times daily) to reduce equinus, which increases tibial nerve tension during gait.
Take neuropathic analgesics at the prescribed time; gabapentin and pregabalin are most effective when taken consistently rather than as-needed.
Keep a symptom diary recording pain intensity, triggering activities, and rest intervals to share with the specialist at follow-up.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) to reduce perineural inflammation
B vitamins (particularly B12 from meat, dairy, eggs) to support peripheral nerve function and myelin maintenance
Magnesium-rich foods (leafy greens, nuts, legumes) for nerve conduction support
Adequate protein intake to support tissue repair during rehabilitation
avoid: Excess alcohol, which is neurotoxic and exacerbates peripheral neuropathy that may coexist with tarsal tunnel syndrome
High-sugar diet in diabetic patients, worsening underlying neuropathy and systemic inflammation
Excess sodium, promoting lower-extremity edema that worsens tunnel pressure

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling) maintains fitness without prolonged weight-bearing loading of the tarsal tunnel. Running and prolonged walking on hard surfaces worsen symptoms during the active phase; these can resume once symptoms are controlled. Post-surgical patients begin range-of-motion exercises at 2 weeks, gentle weight-bearing at 2-4 weeks depending on procedure, and return to sport at 8-16 weeks after nerve decompression.
dailyManagement: Insert orthotics in footwear before first weight-bearing activity each day.
Perform calf and tibial nerve mobilization stretches as prescribed — nerve gliding exercises gently mobilize the tibial nerve through its tunnel to reduce adhesions.
Apply ice pack to medial ankle and plantar foot for 15-20 minutes after peak activity.
Take neuropathic analgesic (gabapentin or pregabalin) at prescribed times, including bedtime dose if nocturnal symptoms are present.
Elevate the foot during rest periods to reduce venous congestion within the tarsal tunnel.
Keep a symptom log documenting pain, distribution, and aggravating activities to bring to specialist review.
prognosis: Prognosis in tarsal tunnel syndrome depends strongly on the underlying cause. Lesion-driven cases (ganglion, varicosity, tenosynovitis) achieve good-to-excellent outcomes in 80-90% of surgical cases when the lesion is completely excised and the retinaculum released. Structural (flat-foot-driven) cases respond well when the flat foot deformity is simultaneously corrected. Idiopathic tarsal tunnel syndrome has the most variable prognosis: 50-70% of patients improve after surgery, but up to 30% experience persistent or recurrent symptoms. Conservative treatment achieves satisfactory control in 50-60% of mild-to-moderate cases over 3-6 months; however, if symptoms represent an expanding mass lesion, delay in surgery allows progressive nerve fiber loss. The predictive factors for good surgical outcome include short symptom duration (under 12 months), identifiable compressive lesion on MRI, positive Tinel's sign, positive response to diagnostic nerve block, and confirmatory electrodiagnostic findings. Concurrent diabetic neuropathy significantly reduces surgical success rates to approximately 50%.
recoveryTimeline: Conservative: orthotic fitting and physiotherapy produce symptom improvement over 4-12 weeks in responders. Steroid injection: peak effect at 2-3 weeks, lasting 6-12 weeks. Surgical decompression: pain improvement often begins within 2-4 weeks postoperatively; neurological recovery (sensory and motor) continues for 3-12 months depending on axonal damage. Return to normal footwear at 4-6 weeks, full unrestricted activity at 8-16 weeks.
complications: Persistent or recurrent neurological symptoms after surgery (incomplete release or scar formation re-entrapment), occurring in 15-25% of idiopathic cases; revision decompression is more complex and less successful than primary surgery.
Wound healing complications — the medial ankle incision is under tension; wound dehiscence and hematoma occur in approximately 2-5% of cases.
Injury to the medial calcaneal or sural nerve branches during surgery, producing a painful neuroma at the incision site.
Reflex sympathetic dystrophy (complex regional pain syndrome) — rare but severe complication with worsening burning pain, swelling, and allodynia after surgery; requires specialist pain management.
Progressive intrinsic muscle atrophy from prolonged nerve compression — if motor axons are irreversibly lost before decompression, weakness and altered foot mechanics may persist despite surgery.
Recurrence or progression of the underlying cause (e.g., flat foot worsening, regrowth of ganglion) without address of structural drivers.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association — Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of tarsal tunnel syndrome causes, diagnosis, and treatment.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Foot and Ankle Society — Tarsal Tunnel
supportResources.description: Orthopaedic perspective on surgical decompression outcomes and patient expectations.

supportResources.name: NHS UK — Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
supportResources.description: National Health Service UK patient information on symptoms, when to seek care, and treatment pathways.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis causes sharp inferior heel pain specifically on first steps in the morning and at the heel insertion that is not typically burning or tingling. Tarsal tunnel syndrome produces burning, numbness, and tingling distributed across the plantar foot, aggravated by prolonged activity rather than isolated to morning first steps. Tinel's sign is present in tarsal tunnel; not in plantar fasciitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Neuropathic pain from systemic causes produces symmetric, bilateral, length-dependent burning and numbness in both feet progressing from toes to ankles. Tarsal tunnel syndrome is typically unilateral (or asymmetric) and localized to the tibial nerve territory. Nerve conduction studies distinguish focal entrapment from diffuse polyneuropathy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Adult Flatfoot / PTTD
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Adult-acquired flat foot from PTTD causes medial arch and tibialis posterior tendon pain rather than burning or tingling. However, severe flat-foot deformity can stretch the tibial nerve at the tarsal tunnel causing secondary tarsal tunnel syndrome — both diagnoses should be considered in a flat-footed patient with plantar neurological symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common differential diagnosis; both cause medial heel and arch pain, though mechanisms differ — plantar fasciitis is a fascial tendinopathy while tarsal tunnel is a nerve entrapment.

relatedConditions.name: Flat Feet (Pes Planus)
relatedConditions.relevance: Hindfoot valgus in flat feet is the most common structural driver of tarsal tunnel compression; treating flat feet is integral to managing this subtype of tarsal tunnel syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Neuropathic pain from diabetic or idiopathic polyneuropathy often coexists with tarsal tunnel syndrome in a double-crush phenomenon; both diagnoses should be evaluated simultaneously.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetes is the most common systemic condition predisposing to tarsal tunnel syndrome via peripheral neuropathy and neuropathic joint changes.

relatedConditions.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Anatomically analogous nerve entrapment at the wrist; patients may have concurrent carpal and tarsal tunnel involvement from systemic conditions (rheumatoid arthritis, diabetes, hypothyroidism).

faqs.question: What is tarsal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is compression of the posterior tibial nerve as it passes through the tarsal tunnel — a fibro-osseous canal behind and below the medial ankle bone. It is the foot's equivalent of carpal tunnel syndrome. Symptoms include burning, tingling, and numbness along the plantar foot, made worse by prolonged standing and relieved by rest.

faqs.question: What does tarsal tunnel syndrome feel like?
faqs.answer: The characteristic sensation is burning, tingling, or electric-shock pain along the bottom of the foot, extending from the medial heel to the toes. Patients often describe walking on sand, a hot iron, or electric shocks. Symptoms worsen throughout the day and after prolonged activity, and may cause nocturnal tingling or aching.

faqs.question: What causes tarsal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are space-occupying lesions within the tarsal tunnel (ganglion cysts, varicose veins, lipomas), flat-foot deformity stretching the tibial nerve, post-traumatic fibrosis after ankle injury, and inflammatory tenosynovitis of adjacent tendons. Approximately 20-25% of cases are idiopathic with no identifiable cause.

faqs.question: How is tarsal tunnel syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines clinical examination — Tinel's test over the tarsal tunnel reproducing plantar tingling, and sensory deficit mapping — with nerve conduction studies confirming slowing across the tibial nerve. MRI identifies space-occupying lesions. A diagnostic nerve block that relieves symptoms confirms the nerve as the pain source.

faqs.question: Is tarsal tunnel syndrome the same as plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: No. Plantar fasciitis causes sharp inferior heel pain on first steps in the morning from fascial inflammation. Tarsal tunnel syndrome causes burning, numbness, and tingling throughout the plantar foot from nerve compression. Both can produce medial heel pain, but tarsal tunnel has neurological symptoms and a positive Tinel's sign at the medial ankle.

faqs.question: Can tarsal tunnel syndrome be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, for mild-to-moderate cases. Custom orthotics correcting flat feet, corticosteroid injection into the tarsal tunnel, NSAIDs, and neuropathic pain medications control symptoms in 50-60% of patients. Conservative treatment should be optimized for 3-6 months before surgical referral is considered.

faqs.question: What surgery is done for tarsal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Tibial nerve decompression involves dividing the flexor retinaculum over the tarsal tunnel to release pressure on the nerve and its branches. Any space-occupying lesion (ganglion, varicosity) is also removed. Good-to-excellent outcomes occur in 80-90% when a specific lesion is found, and in 50-70% of idiopathic cases.

faqs.question: How long does tarsal tunnel syndrome take to heal?
faqs.answer: With conservative treatment, symptoms improve over 4-12 weeks in responders. After surgical decompression, early pain relief begins within 2-4 weeks; full neurological recovery takes 3-12 months depending on the degree of prior nerve damage. Patients with longer symptom duration before surgery have slower and less complete recovery.

faqs.question: Can flat feet cause tarsal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hindfoot valgus from flat feet elongates the tarsal tunnel and stretches the tibial nerve during the hyperpronated gait cycle. Flat-foot-related tarsal tunnel is often bilateral and responds to correction of the deformity with orthotics or reconstructive foot surgery in addition to standard tarsal tunnel decompression.

faqs.question: What is the Tinel's sign for tarsal tunnel?
faqs.answer: A positive Tinel's sign for tarsal tunnel syndrome is reproduction of burning or tingling in the plantar foot when the posterior tibial nerve is tapped behind and below the medial malleolus. It is not 100% sensitive but, when positive, strongly supports the diagnosis. Up to 60% of confirmed cases have a positive Tinel's sign on examination.

faqs.question: Can tarsal tunnel syndrome cause permanent nerve damage?
faqs.answer: Prolonged untreated tarsal tunnel compression can cause irreversible axonal loss, resulting in permanent plantar numbness and intrinsic foot muscle weakness. Early decompression — before motor axons degenerate — gives the best neurological recovery. This is why progressive symptoms despite conservative treatment should prompt timely surgical assessment.

faqs.question: Does diabetes cause tarsal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Diabetes does not directly cause tarsal tunnel syndrome, but pre-existing diabetic peripheral neuropathy makes the tibial nerve more vulnerable to focal compression at the tarsal tunnel — a double-crush effect. Diabetic patients with tarsal tunnel compression have lower surgical cure rates (around 50%) than non-diabetic patients.

faqs.question: Are orthotics helpful for tarsal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Custom foot orthotics with medial heel posting reduce tibial nerve tension by correcting hindfoot valgus in flat-foot-related tarsal tunnel syndrome. They are most effective in the structural subtype. Studies show orthotics reduce tarsal tunnel pressure significantly in valgus deformity; they are less effective in lesion-driven or idiopathic cases.

faqs.question: Can I run with tarsal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Running with active tarsal tunnel syndrome worsens nerve compression and delays recovery. During the acute phase, switch to low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling). After symptoms are controlled with conservative treatment or surgery, a gradual return to running with appropriate orthotics and footwear can begin under podiatric guidance.

faqs.question: What is the success rate of tarsal tunnel surgery?
faqs.answer: Success rates depend on the underlying cause: 80-90% good-to-excellent outcomes when a specific compressive lesion is removed, versus 50-70% for idiopathic cases. The best predictors of surgical success are a short symptom duration, a positive diagnostic nerve block, positive electrodiagnostics, and an identifiable mass on preoperative MRI.
